analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 6 finding(s) from 8 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: Debug unit can write to all CSRs without authentication, bypassing any expected software-only write protection.
findings.vulnerability_category: CSR write enable / any CSR register (e.g., mstatus, mie, mip) / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: csr_req_n set when debug_halted_o and we_i
mux selects CSR operation from debug, no additional guard
CSR write logic uses csr_we_int without lock or auth check

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: debug_unit Operation: CSR write enable Object: any CSR register (e.g., mstatus, mie, mip) Expected guard: debug authentication and/or hardware lock bits preventing debug modification of critical CSRs Observed behavior: Debug unit can write CSRs when core is halted, without any authentication or lock check Path: debug_unit -> zeroriscy_core mux (csr_access, csr_addr, csr_wdata, csr_op) -> zeroriscy_cs_registers Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: unauthorized modification of privileged CSRs, potential privilege escalation
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: No authentication mechanism, no lock bits found. Uncertainty about CSRs that may contain security-sensitive fields; no security-sensitivity label.

findings.summary: Debug unit can read all GPRs and CSRs while core halted, potentially exposing cryptographic keys or other secrets.
findings.vulnerability_category: read GPR/CSR / general-purpose registers (register_file) and CSRs / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: regfile_rreq set when halted, csr read path

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: debug_unit Operation: read GPR/CSR Object: general-purpose registers (register_file) and CSRs Expected guard: debug authentication or read-enable bit after proper authorization Observed behavior: Debug unit can read GPRs and CSRs when core halted without any auth check Path: debug_unit -> regfile read port (regfile_rreq_o, regfile_raddr_o) -> register_file or csr read via csr_req_o Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: exposure of confidential CPU state (GPRs, CSRs)
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: No authentication present; all debug reads are permitted when halted.

findings.summary: Potential bypass of secure interrupt configuration.
findings.vulnerability_category: write to interrupt controller configuration registers / interrupt enables, secure status, priority registers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: any APB master (CPU, debug, DMA) Operation: write to interrupt controller configuration registers Object: interrupt enables, secure status, priority registers Expected guard: privilege-level or secure-mode check (core_secure_mode_i) restricting writes to trusted initiators Observed behavior: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module does not gate write accesses with core_secure_mode_i in the visible RTL; security parameter ENA_SEC_IRQ exists but its implementation not fully observed. Path: APB bus -> apb_interrupt_cntrl register map -> register write logic (r_mask, r_int, etc.) Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: unauthorized reconfiguration of interrupts or enabling secure interrupts by non-secure master
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The module may include secure gating using ENA_SEC_IRQ in later lines (truncated). Need to review full file to confirm.

findings.summary: Address aliasing exists, may bypass intended address-based protection.
findings.vulnerability_category: memory/peripheral access via alternative address / protected peripheral (e.g., secure ROM, debug control) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: any bus master Operation: memory/peripheral access via alternative address Object: protected peripheral (e.g., secure ROM, debug control) Expected guard: non-overlapping address decode, no aliasing, proper security attribute enforcement in demux Observed behavior: Address map defines aliases (e.g., ALIAS_SOC_L2_PRI_CH0_SCM at 0x0000_6000) and CLUSTER_ALIAS/FC_ALIAS macros, potentially allowing bypass of address-based protections. Path: fc_demux uses port_sel_i to route, no access control; aliases could route to restricted regions through unexpected ports. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: access to restricted peripheral or memory region via alias
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Exact memory protection architecture not clear; potential aliasing may be intentional and protected by other means.

findings.summary: Debug exit does not sanitize CSRs, allowing persistent privilege escalation from debug session.
findings.vulnerability_category: state retention after debug exit / privileged CSRs (mstatus, mie, mip, etc.) / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: stall control FSM transitions, no CSR update
no CSR restoration on debug exit
CSR write logic, no automatic save/restore on debug transition

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: hardware transition from debug mode to normal mode Operation: state retention after debug exit Object: privileged CSRs (mstatus, mie, mip, etc.) Expected guard: automatic clearing or restoration of CSRs upon debug exit to prevent privilege escalation Observed behavior: Debug exit simply changes stall state; no CSR restoration logic. Any CSR modifications made during debug persist. Path: debug exit via debug_resume_i -> controller FSM -> stall deassert; CSR registers remain unchanged. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: privilege escalation: debug-modified privileged bits (e.g., mstatus.mie) are not cleared, giving normal software elevated privileges.

findings.summary: Potential information leakage of secure interrupt status.
findings.vulnerability_category: read secured interrupt status or configuration / secure interrupt IDs and state (via core_irq_sec_o or registers) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: non-secure software/bus master Operation: read secured interrupt status or configuration Object: secure interrupt IDs and state (via core_irq_sec_o or registers) Expected guard: core_secure_mode_i must gate reads of secure data; secure interrupt information must not be exposed to non-secure reads Observed behavior: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module likely does not mask secure interrupt information from non-secure reads. Visible code does not show gating of APB read data based on core_secure_mode_i. Path: APB read data path -> register read mux -> core_irq_sec_o or other status outputs Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: information leak of secure interrupt state to non-secure master
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The module may contain secure masking logic in the truncated part (line 135+). Need to confirm.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
